medications.indication_rationale: Permethrin 5% cream is the first-line scabicide and the definitive treatment that eradicates the mites. It must be applied to the WHOLE body from the jawline/neck down (with particular attention to the finger-webs, wrists, umbilicus, genitalia and under the nails), left on 8-14 hours, then washed off, and repeated once after 7 days to kill newly hatched mites. CRITICAL: every household contact must be treated SIMULTANEOUSLY on the same night, whether or not they are itching, to break transmission. Adult patient — dose taken directly from the source note; no paediatric dosing involved.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 5% cream topically: apply to the whole body from the jawline down, leave on 8-14 hours then wash off; repeat once after 7 days

medications.indication_rationale: A sedating antihistamine at night controls the distressing pruritus and, importantly, restores sleep after 72 hours of severe sleep deprivation. It treats the symptom, not the mites. Dose taken directly from the source note; adult patient.
medications.duration: short course at night while the itch settles

medications.indication_rationale: Oral ivermectin is an acceptable alternative or adjunct scabicide (e.g. where topical treatment is impractical, for widespread/resistant disease, or to help treat multiple contacts), but topical permethrin is first-line for classic scabies in an immunocompetent adult, so ivermectin is not required here. It should not be given to young children or in pregnancy.

Applying permethrin on multiple consecutive nights to chase the ongoing itch is a harmful error of commission — it causes irritant dermatitis and burning of already-excoriated skin. The itch that persists after treatment is post-scabetic hypersensitivity to dead mites, not live infestation, and is managed with antihistamines and emollients, NOT more scabicide. Only the single 7-day repeat application is correct.

No established secondary infection at presentation — the patient is afebrile and systemically well with no honey-coloured crusting, fluctuance, spreading erythema or lymphangitic streaking. Empirical systemic antibiotics are not indicated and would be antibiotic overuse; reserve them for signs of impetiginisation/cellulitis, which are explicitly safety-netted.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Treat EVERY member of the household on the same night as the patient — including the spouse and anyone else living there — whether or not they are itching, and repeat the whole-household treatment after 7 days.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Simultaneous whole-household treatment is the single most important step to eradicate the mites and prevent re-infestation ping-pong between contacts.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Hot-wash (or hot-dry / seal in a bag for 72 hours) all bedding, towels and recently worn clothing the morning after treatment.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Environmental decontamination removes mites shed onto fabrics and prevents re-infestation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Reassure the patient that the itch will persist for 2-4 weeks after successful treatment (post-scabetic itch) and manage it with the antihistamine and emollients rather than more cream.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Understanding the expected clinical course prevents anxiety-driven over-application of scabicide and unnecessary re-presentation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the raw, scratched areas clean and avoid further scratching; keep fingernails short.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces further skin trauma and lowers the risk of secondary bacterial infection of the excoriations.

patient_education_points.point: This is a treatable skin-mite infestation. A cream applied over the whole body tonight kills the mites; the key is that everyone in the house is treated on the same night.

The itch will continue for 2-4 weeks even after the mites are dead — that is your immune system reacting to them, not a treatment failure. Do NOT keep re-applying the cream night after night; use the anti-itch tablets instead.
A second application of the cream in exactly 7 days is essential to kill any newly hatched mites.
Because the skin is scratched raw and bleeding, it could become infected — watch for spreading redness, heat, swelling, thick yellow crust or pus, or a fever.
Wash all bedding, towels and recently worn clothes in a hot wash the morning after treatment.

follow_up_plan.interval: Routine GP review in about 2 weeks (sooner if any escalation trigger appears)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Resolution of the infestation, any persistent post-scabetic nodules or itch, whether household contacts were treated, and any signs of secondary infection.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spreading redness, warmth or swelling, thick yellow crust or pus, or fever — same-day/urgent review; systemic unwellness or red streaks tracking from the skin — go to the emergency department.

must_include_items: Recognise the classic infestation from the nocturnal itch + flexural/genital distribution + an affected close household contact
Prescribe/recommend a topical scabicide (permethrin 5% cream) applied whole-body from the jawline down, left 8-14 hours, repeated once after 7 days
Instruct SIMULTANEOUS treatment of ALL household contacts on the same night, whether or not they itch
Advise environmental decontamination — hot-wash bedding, towels and recently worn clothing
Provide a sedating antihistamine (or equivalent) for the itch and to restore sleep
Counsel that post-treatment itch persists 2-4 weeks and must NOT be treated with repeated scabicide application
Arrange routine GP review and give clear secondary-infection safety-netting
acceptable_alternatives: Oral ivermectin as an alternative/adjunct scabicide (not in young children or pregnancy) instead of or alongside topical permethrin
An alternative sedating antihistamine (e.g. an OTC sedating antihistamine) instead of promethazine for the night-time itch
Sealing non-washable items in a bag for ~72 hours instead of hot-washing
errors_of_omission: Treating only the index patient and omitting whole-household simultaneous treatment
No secondary-infection safety-netting for the raw, bleeding skin
Failing to address the disabling itch and sleep loss
errors_of_commission: Escalating an afebrile, systemically-well patient to ED or calling an ambulance for classic scabies with no infection signs
Advising repeated nightly application of permethrin to chase the residual itch
Prescribing systemic antibiotics with no established infection
Relying on antihistamines alone without a scabicide
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising scabies, a topical scabicide with whole-household simultaneous treatment + 7-day repeat, itch/sleep relief, environmental decontamination, and secondary-infection safety-netting. This is NOT an emergency — over-triage to ED/000 is a scored defect, and do NOT require emergency escalation for a pass.
